notes: Phase 2a adjudication re-opened six Phase-1 disputed/anachronistic items in ch.4 and issued dual rulings: all six fail as written (five ruled 'improbable' on a single fact component, one — GC-04-005 — improbable on tied fact/motive components), while five of six weakened readings recover to well-supported and one (GC-04-069) recovers only to probable, and one (GC-04-113) recovers only to probable. This confirms Phase-1's own diagnosis that the chapter's errors cluster in embellishment, absoluteness, and causal-priority overreach layered on largely genuine kernels (real book-destruction practices, real clandestine Scripture transmission, real Waldensian rejection of merit-machinery, real Waldensian-Hussite Bohemian networks), not in fabrication of events. The two highest-impact items remain the chapter's explicit Reformation hand-off (GC-04-113, score 12) and the soteriology claim underwriting the conversion-drama core (GC-04-082, score 9); the three student-missionary claims (GC-04-069/070/071) form a low-to-moderate-impact decorative/embellishment cluster. No claim in this batch is load-bearing: the chapter's actual identity case rests on the antiquity and Sabbath pillars, which lie outside this six-claim rescore.

GC-04-005.reasoning: Tied fact/motive failure: no located conciliar decree targets 'records of Rome's cruelty' as such — documented decrees state heresy or blasphemy as their object — and no documentation supports an evidence-suppression motive against the rival documented motive (heresy enforcement). Framing_gap=3 means the weakened reading (dissenters' own writings, including implicit persecution accounts, perished alongside heretical corpora) is well-supported; the as-written failure converts a real effect into an undocumented specific intent, an independent evidentiary failure not inherited from any other claim. It sits in the chapter's opening 'why so little record survives' framing rather than in the antiquity or Sabbath pillars the chapter's identity case rests on, so deleting it costs the argument-from-silence one supporting brick but leaves ¶1's parallel language and the chapter's well-attested persecution record intact.

GC-04-069.reasoning: Fact failure: no medieval attestation (matriculation record, deposition, barbe manuscript) supports Waldensian youth studying at city institutions in France or Italy; the claim traces through Wylie to post-1532 Vaudois pastor-historians retrojecting a Protestant self-image, while modern archival scholarship (Audisio, Cameron, Biller) documents a deliberately clandestine, non-university artisan formation. Framing_gap=2 (weakened only to 'probable') shows even the recoverable substance is not as solidly attested as the failed claim implies. This is an independent evidentiary failure, not inherited, though it forms the unattested premise GC-04-070 and GC-04-071 separately build their own embellishments on; deleting it leaves the chapter's well-documented itinerant merchant-missionary narrative (¶24-26) intact and removes only this one romanticized schooling vignette.

GC-04-070.reasoning: Fact failure: the tailored-garment concealment device has no attestation in any primary source or modern scholarship, though the underlying practice — Scripture concealed on the person or among goods while traveling — is well documented (Audisio's barbe corpus, Lea, Wylie). Framing_gap=3 signals the substance fully survives at the weakened level; the as-written failure is one invented physical detail welded to GC-04-069's unattested premise, not a misrepresentation of the broader documented practice. Independent evidentiary failure, purely decorative in function. Deleting this detail costs the narrative no argumentative weight — it is embellishment on an otherwise solid claim about clandestine Scripture transmission.

GC-04-071.reasoning: Fact failure on two counts: the school-conversion scene is wholly derivative of the unattested GC-04-069 premise and unattested itself (even Wylie's version has converts among merchants and landlords, not whole schools), and the absolute 'could not, by the closest inquiry, trace' claim is positively contradicted by documented successful inquisitions (Strasbourg 1212, Secco 1387-88, Cologne 1392, the Pomeranian trials of 1392-94) rather than merely undocumented — a stronger failure than a plain evidence gap. Framing_gap=3 shows the weakened reading (clandestine evangelism that often evaded detection for long periods) is well-supported. Independent evidentiary failure; deleting it removes a flattering but factually contradicted picture of Rome's incompetence, while the persecution narrative in ¶40-45 already carries the chapter's argument without this specific untraceability claim.

GC-04-082.reasoning: Fact failure: specialist scholarship is essentially unanimous that medieval Waldensian soteriology was penitential rather than sola fide — barbes heard confession and assigned works of satisfaction — so the claim as printed, embedded in paragraph 29's grace-alone rhetorical frame ('Christ as their only hope... reliance upon human merit intercepts'), retrojects a post-1532 Protestant confession onto the medieval movement. Framing_gap=3 means the narrow, weakened reading (rejection of purgatory, indulgences, and suffrages for the dead, 'God alone pardons sin') is well-supported and real; the as-written failure is specifically the absolutized grace-alone soteriology, not the underlying rejection of merit-machinery. Independent evidentiary failure feeding the chapter's emotional core (the conversion drama of ¶28-38) rather than the antiquity or Sabbath pillars the identity case rests on; delete it and the conversion narrative loses its doctrinal anchor but the chapter's argument does not collapse, since the well-documented machinery-rejection can still carry comparable rhetorical weight in narrower form.

GC-04-113.reasoning: Fact failure: mainstream modern scholarship reverses the sentence's causal claim — Wycliffe's dissent grew from Oxford scholastic theology and Luther's from Augustinian academic theology, with no demonstrated Waldensian textual input into either — so the Wycliffe/Luther seeding-genealogy fails as written. Framing_gap=2 (weakened only to 'probable') reflects genuine ongoing debate: Molnar/Gonnet's 'First Reformation' continuity school and documented Waldensian-Hussite networks (Friedrich Reiser, the Unitas Fratrum's 1467 recourse to a Waldensian bishop) support a real contributory role in the Bohemian reform stream specifically, not a channel reaching Wycliffe or Luther. Independent evidentiary failure sitting precisely at the chapter's closing hand-off to the book's central thesis chain (persecuted minorities to Reformation); centrality is high because this sentence performs that structural bridging function even though its specific causal claim fails. Delete it and the chapter's own internal argument about the Waldenses is untouched — only the explicit transition to the Wycliffe material would need restating in less absolute terms.
